title: Principles of Immunization
authors: Kathleen M. Neuzil, Kathryn M. Edwards

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.title: Impact of Vaccination Programs on Global Health
items.content: Vaccination programs have significantly reduced mortality rates globally by preventing infectious diseases.
keyPoints: Vaccines averted more than 37 million deaths globally between 2000 and 2019.
This represents a 45% reduction in deaths compared with a scenario of no vaccination.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.title: Mechanism of Vaccine-Induced Immunity
items.content: Vaccines stimulate the immune system to produce a protective response, either through active or passive immunity.
keyPoints: Active immunity is induced by vaccines containing inactivated or attenuated pathogens or components like nucleic acids and proteins.
Passive immunity involves the introduction of antibodies from another source, such as monoclonal antibodies for SARS-CoV-2.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.title: Maternal Immunization and Passive Antibody Transfer
items.content: Vaccinating pregnant individuals can confer passive immunity to the fetus through antibody transfer.
keyPoints: Examples include vaccines for tetanus, acellular pertussis, RSV, influenza, and COVID-19.
These vaccines protect both the mother and the baby by transferring antibodies via the placenta.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.title: Evolution of Vaccine Formulations
items.content: Vaccine formulations have evolved significantly from whole-cell and toxoid vaccines to recombinant DNA and mRNA technologies. This evolution has been driven by advances in biotechnology, molecular genetics, and the urgent public health needs such as the COVID-19 pandemic.
keyPoints: Early vaccines included whole-cell typhoid, cholera, diphtheria, and tetanus toxoids.
Live attenuated vaccines developed from 1950 to 1980 include poliomyelitis, measles, mumps, rubella, and varicella.
Conjugate vaccines like pneumococcal, Haemophilus influenzae type b, and meningococcal were developed to improve immune responses.
Recombinant DNA technology led to hepatitis B and HPV vaccines.
COVID-19 accelerated the development of mRNA and vector-based vaccine technologies.

items.title: Role and Types of Adjuvants in Vaccines
items.content: Adjuvants are crucial for enhancing immune responses, especially in inactivated or protein-based vaccines. They range from early emulsions to modern Toll-like receptor agonists.
keyPoints: Replicating viral vaccines typically do not require adjuvants.
Alum has been used safely for over 90 years and is common in pediatric vaccines.
Oil-in-water emulsions like MF59 and AS03 contain squalene and are used with influenza vaccines.
Toll-like receptor agonists such as TLR4 (MPL) and TLR9 (CpG) are included in HPV and hepatitis B vaccines, respectively.
LNPs not only deliver mRNA but also have an adjuvanting effect.

items.title: Technological Advances in Vaccine Development
items.content: Recent technological advances, particularly during the COVID-19 pandemic, have revolutionized vaccine development with mRNA and vector-based platforms.
keyPoints: mRNA technology was enhanced by lipid nanoparticle (LNP) delivery systems.
Adjuvants like MPL combined with QS-21 are used in shingles and malaria vaccines.
Matrix-M is an adjuvant approved for COVID-19 and R21 malaria vaccines.
Innovations include real-time safety surveillance, trial design, and immunologic correlates of protection.

items.title: Establishing the Need for an Immunization
items.content: Before developing vaccines, it's crucial to establish the disease burden and population at risk. This is often done using surveillance systems like those of the CDC. In some cases, vaccines are developed based on theoretical risks, such as potential pandemic threats.
keyPoints: Establishing disease burden and population at risk is essential before vaccine development.
Surveillance systems (e.g., CDC) measure disease burden and at-risk populations.
Vaccines may be developed for theoretical risks like pandemic influenza strains.

items.title: Phases of Vaccine Development
items.content: Vaccine development progresses through preclinical stages and three clinical phases: Phase 1, 2, and 3. Controlled human infection models (CHIM) may be used when phase 3 trials are delayed or challenging.
keyPoints: Vaccine development includes preclinical studies and three clinical phases.
Phase 1 involves fewer than 100 healthy participants to test safety and immunogenicity.
Controlled human infection models (CHIM) can be part of the pathway under specific circumstances.

items.title: Phase 1 Clinical Trials
items.content: After preclinical studies show a vaccine stimulates an immune response without toxicity, phase 1 trials begin. In the U.S., an Investigational New Drug (IND) application must be submitted and approved by the FDA before these trials start.
keyPoints: Phase 1 trials test safety and immunogenicity in fewer than 100 healthy participants aged 18-55.
An IND application is required and must be approved by the FDA before phase 1 trials.
Participants are well informed about risks and benefits, and undergo rigorous safety assessments.

items.title: Understanding Phase 1 Vaccine Trials
items.content: Phase 1 vaccine trials focus on safety, tolerability, and immunogenicity in a small group of participants. They involve dose-ranging studies starting with the lowest doses, increasing if tolerated, and are monitored for adverse events.
keyPoints: Dose-ranging component starts with the lowest vaccine doses.
Independent Data Safety and Monitoring Committees (DSMCs) oversee safety.
Halting rules include severe reactions or hospitalization due to the vaccine.
Immunologic studies assess antibody responses and T lymphocyte responses.

items.title: Role of Phase 3 Vaccine Trials
items.content: Phase 3 trials are pivotal in determining vaccine efficacy by preventing a predefined endpoint, typically laboratory-confirmed disease. They involve randomization and blinding to ensure unbiased results.
keyPoints: Randomized and blinded study design with placebo control.
Primary goal is to determine vaccine efficacy against the pathogen.
Serologic correlates of protection may be identified if feasible.
Lot-consistency assessments are required before licensure.

items.title: Utilization of Controlled Human Infection Models (CHIMs) in Vaccine Development
items.content: CHIMs involve intentionally infecting participants to study vaccine efficacy and immunologic responses, offering a rapid pathway for candidate selection and approval.
keyPoints: CHIMs provide unique insights into correlates of protection.
Used for downselection of candidates or informing vaccine approval.
Examples include vaccines for cholera, influenza, typhoid fever, shigellosis, and malaria.
FDA consultation is required to determine the use of CHIM.

items.title: FDA Licensure and VRBPAC Role
items.content: The FDA requires comprehensive data from phase 3 trials for vaccine licensure, consulting the Vaccine and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee (VRBPAC) to evaluate efficacy and safety. The FDA makes independent licensing decisions based on this guidance.
keyPoints: FDA reviews phase 3 trial data for licensure.
VRBPAC advises on vaccine efficacy and safety.
Significant safety concerns can delay licensure.
Children and pregnant women require specific studies for inclusion.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)

items.title: ACIP's Role in Vaccine Recommendations
items.content: Post-licensure, the ACIP determines vaccine use recommendations and issues annual schedules. It considers disease epidemiology, vaccine efficacy/safety, and evidence quality to guide immunization practices.
keyPoints: ACIP provides vaccination recommendations post-licensure.
Annual vaccination schedule issued for all age groups.
Recommendations consider disease burden, vaccine safety, and effectiveness.
ACIP can revise recommendations based on new data.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)

items.title: Postlicensure Surveillance and Vaccine Policy
items.content: After licensure, ongoing monitoring of vaccine effectiveness and safety is essential. This includes assessing waning immunity, identifying at-risk groups for vaccine failure, and evaluating causally related adverse events.
keyPoints: Ongoing surveillance evaluates vaccine effectiveness and safety post-licensure.
Monitoring helps identify waning immunity and need for boosters.
Surveillance assesses rare adverse events not detected pre-licensure.
Disease surveillance determines risk factors and vaccine failure.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.question: Which type of vaccine generally requires only one or two doses to generate a robust immune response?
items.explanation: Live, attenuated vaccines generally require only one or two doses to generate a robust immune response.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.question: What is the primary purpose of adding adjuvants to vaccines?
items.explanation: Adjuvants are added to vaccines to nonspecifically boost the immune response.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.question: What type of immunity is conferred by monoclonal antibodies against SARS-CoV-2?
items.explanation: Monoclonal antibodies provide passive immunity by introducing exogenous antibodies.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.question: What is the role of maternal immunization during pregnancy?
options: To induce active immunity in the fetus
To transfer antibodies from mother to fetus
items.explanation: Maternal immunization transfers antibodies generated by the mother's immune system to the fetus through the placenta.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.question: Which of the following vaccine types was developed using repeated passage of organisms in unnatural hosts?
items.explanation: Repeated passage of organisms in unnatural hosts led to the development of avirulent strains used in the BCG vaccine for tuberculosis and yellow fever vaccines.

items.question: What technological advancement significantly improved mRNA-based vaccine delivery?
items.explanation: The lipid nanoparticle (LNP) delivery system improved the stability and efficiency of in vivo transfection for mRNA vaccines.

items.question: Which adjuvant is combined with alum to form AS04 used in HPV vaccines?
items.explanation: AS04, used in HPV vaccines, combines monophosphoryl lipid A (MPL) with alum.

items.question: Which of the following adjuvants is a saponin-based component approved for use in recombinant COVID-19 and R21 malaria vaccines?
items.explanation: Matrix-M is a saponin-based adjuvant approved for use in recombinant COVID-19 and R21 malaria vaccines.

items.question: What is the first step in establishing the need for an immunization?
options: Measuring vaccine uptake and safety post-implementation
items.explanation: The first step is establishing the burden of disease, the population at risk, and the mechanisms involved in protection.

items.question: What must be demonstrated in preclinical studies for a vaccine to proceed further?
options: Generation of immunity against the pathogen
items.explanation: Immunity generation must be demonstrated in preclinical studies for further testing.

items.question: What is the primary focus of phase 1 clinical trials?
options: Testing safety and immunogenicity in a small group
Assessing long-term impact on population health
items.explanation: Phase 1 trials primarily test the safety of the new experimental vaccine, although immunogenicity is also measured.

items.question: What must a vaccine manufacturer submit to the FDA before starting phase 1 studies?
items.explanation: The manufacturer must submit an IND application to the FDA before phase 1 trials can begin.

items.question: What is the primary purpose of Phase 1 vaccine studies?
options: To determine if the vaccine prevents disease in large populations.
To assess safety and immunogenicity with dose-ranging components.
To compare the vaccine against a placebo in randomized trials.
To establish lot consistency for vaccine production.
items.explanation: Phase 1 studies involve assessing safety, tolerability, and initial immune responses, often using dose-ranging to determine safe levels.

items.question: What role do independent Data Safety and Monitoring Committees (DSMCs) play in Phase 1 studies?
options: They conduct the immunologic studies on participants.
They assess reactions and approve dose escalation based on safety.
They design the study protocols for vaccine trials.
They are responsible for administering vaccines to participants.
items.explanation: DSMCs independently review safety data and determine if it is safe to increase the dose levels in Phase 1 studies.

items.question: What is a key feature of Phase 3 vaccine trials?
options: They involve only a small number of subjects.
They are conducted without blinding to the vaccine or control agent.
They aim to determine if vaccines prevent predefined endpoints like disease.
They focus solely on determining immunogenicity.
items.explanation: Phase 3 trials are designed to assess whether vaccines can prevent a specific endpoint, typically disease prevention, with participants blinded to their group assignment.

items.question: What is the purpose of Controlled Human Infection Models (CHIMs) in vaccine development?
options: To replace all phases of clinical trials.
To provide insights into correlates of protection and inform approval.
To conduct large-scale efficacy studies in endemic areas.
To perform long-term safety assessments post-licensure.
items.explanation: CHIMs are used to gain unique immunologic insights, help downselect vaccine candidates for further trials or approvals, and can expedite the development process when traditional trials are impractical.

items.question: Which committee advises the FDA on whether a vaccine should be licensed based on efficacy and safety data?
options: Vaccine and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
items.explanation: The VRBPAC advises the FDA on vaccine licensure decisions.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)

items.question: Which organization provides recommendations for vaccination schedules in the United States?
items.explanation: The ACIP issues yearly vaccination schedules and recommendations.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)

items.question: What is a key role of the Vaccines for Children (VFC) Program?
options: Ensuring health equity by providing vaccines to children who cannot afford them
items.explanation: The VFC Program ensures that all children have access to recommended vaccinations.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)

items.question: What is a critical aspect of postlicensure surveillance?
options: Evaluating vaccine effectiveness and safety in real-world settings
Conducting initial phase trials for new vaccines
Setting up prelicensure clinical trial protocols
items.explanation: Postlicensure surveillance involves evaluating vaccine effectiveness and safety.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.explanation: Vaccines primarily stimulate active immunity by inducing an immune response in the host.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.statement: Live, attenuated vaccines can often achieve protection with fewer doses compared to inactivated vaccines.
items.explanation: Live, attenuated vaccines generally require only one or two doses to generate a robust immune response.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.statement: Replicating viral vaccines generally require the addition of adjuvants to enhance immune responses.
items.explanation: Replicating viral vaccines typically do not need adjuvants, as they inherently stimulate strong immune responses.

items.statement: Alum has been used as an adjuvant in vaccines for over 90 years.
items.explanation: Alum is the oldest adjuvant and has been in use for more than 90 years, promoting humoral immune responses.

items.statement: Controlled human infection models may be used when phase 3 trials are delayed or challenging.
items.explanation: CHIMs can be part of the development pathway under specific circumstances.

items.statement: Phase 1 clinical trials enroll large numbers of participants to test vaccine efficacy.
items.explanation: Phase 1 trials enroll limited numbers, usually fewer than 100 individuals, primarily to test safety.

items.statement: Phase 1 studies often involve several hundred subjects.
items.explanation: Phase 1 studies typically involve a small number of participants, focusing on safety and initial immune responses.

items.statement: CHIMs can be used to establish rapid pathways for vaccine candidate downselection.
items.explanation: Controlled Human Infection Models provide insights that may expedite the selection of promising vaccine candidates.

items.statement: The FDA makes the final decision on whether to license a vaccine, even after consulting with VRBPAC.
items.explanation: The FDA decides independently on vaccine licensure.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)

items.statement: Most adult vaccines in the United States are delivered by public providers.
items.explanation: Most adult vaccines are delivered by private healthcare providers.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.assertion: Monoclonal antibodies are used for prophylaxis in infants and young children.
items.reason: They provide passive immunity by introducing exogenous antibodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.subtopic: Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES
items.reason: Edward Jenner's discovery led to widespread vaccination efforts.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · Principles of Immunization | IMMUNE RESPONSES TO VACCINES

items.assertion: The development of recombinant DNA technology allowed antigens to be expressed in cell lines.
items.reason: This advancement led to the creation of hepatitis B and human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccines.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Oil-in-water emulsions like MF59 contain squalene as a vehicle.
items.reason: They enhance innate and adaptive immune responses in combination with certain influenza vaccines.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Antigen discovery is a crucial step in vaccine development.
items.reason: It involves identifying antigens that can stimulate a protective immune response.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Phase 3 trials are conducted before preclinical studies.
items.reason: Preclinical studies must demonstrate immunity generation and safety.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; phase 3 trials occur after preclinical studies.

items.assertion: Phase 3 trials are necessary to determine vaccine efficacy.
items.reason: They involve large-scale studies with randomized and blinded participants to assess prevention of disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, and the reason correctly explains that Phase 3 trials evaluate vaccine efficacy through rigorous study designs.

items.assertion: Phase 1 studies are designed to confirm vaccine safety in large populations.
items.reason: They involve dose-ranging and immunologic assessments with a small number of participants.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Phase 1 studies focus on initial safety and immune response in a limited number of subjects, not large populations.

items.assertion: The ACIP can revise its vaccine recommendations based on new information.
items.reason: New data on disease epidemiology, effectiveness or safety may prompt revisions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)

items.assertion: The Affordable Care Act mandates that private insurers cover all vaccines.
items.reason: Private insurers are required to cover ACIP-recommended vaccines at in-network providers.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the ACA requires coverage only for ACIP-recommended vaccines.
items.reference: Principles of Immunization · VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 3) | VACCINE DEVELOPMENT (part 4)
